perturbation.original_text: The Buyer acknowledges and agrees that Aldes is, will be an d will remain entitled to receipt of its agreed brokerage commission (albeit due and payable by the Seller/s, the Business/es, the Buyer or any of them ), should the Buyer (albeit directly itself or indirectly t hrough any of its subsidiaries , holding c ompan ies, group c ompanies , related parties and/or inter -related parties or otherwise indirectly) or anyone else the Buyer may have introduced to the Business/es and/or Seller/s , conclude any agreements and/or transactions contemplated in clause s 2.1 and/or 2.2 above with (or with regard to) any Business/es and/or Seller/s .
perturbation.changed_text: The Buyer acknowledges and agrees that Aldes may be entitled to a brokerage commission (albeit due and payable by the Seller/s, the Business/es, the Buyer or any of them), should the Buyer or anyone else the Buyer may have introduced to the Business/es and/or Seller/s, conclude any agreements and/or transactions contemplated in clauses 2.1 and/or 2.2 above with (or with regard to) any Business/es and/or Seller/s, subject to the discretion of the involved parties.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'is, will be and will remain entitled' to 'may be entitled, subject to the discretion of the involved parties' introduces uncertainty. This allows the other parties to decide whether Aldes receives the commission, making it discretionary and unenforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: South African Contract Law - Certainty of Terms
perturbation.law_citation: Case law on the requirement for contracts to have certain and unambiguous terms (e.g., Bredenkamp v Standard Bank of South Africa Ltd 2010 (4) SA 468 (SCA)).
perturbation.law_explanation: South African contract law requires clear and unambiguous terms, especially regarding payment. By adding 'subject to the discretion of the involved parties,' the modification makes the entitlement to the commission uncertain and potentially unenforceable, conflicting with the legal requirement for contractual certainty. The introduction of discretion opens the door for disputes and could render the commission clause invalid.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: contract | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you contract A contract is an agreement between parties , creating mutual obligations that are enforceable by law . The basic elements required for the agreement to be a legally enforceable contract are: mutual assent , expressed by a valid offer and acceptance ; adequate consideration ; capacity ; and legality . In some states , elements of consideration can be satisfied by a valid substitute. Possible remedies for breach of contract include general damages , consequential damages , reliance damages , and specific performance . Background : Contracts are promises that the law will enforce. Contract law is generally governed by state common law , and while general overall contract law is common throughout the country, some specific court interpretations of a particular element of the contract may vary between the states. If a promise is breached, the law provides remedies to the harmed party, often in the form of monetary damages, or in limited circumstances, in the form of specific performance of the promise made. Elements -- Consideration and Mutual Assent Contracts arise when a duty comes into existence, because of a promise made by one of the parties. To be legally binding as a contract, a promise must be exchanged for adequate consideration. There are two different theories or definitions of consideration: Bargain Theory of Consideration and Benefit-Detriment theory of consideration. Benefit-Detriment Under the b enefit-detriment theory, an adequate consideration exists only when a promise made to the benefit of the promisor or to the detriment of the promisee, which reasonably and fairly induces the promisor to make a promise for something else for the promisee. For example, promises that are purely gifts are not considered enforceable because the personal satisfaction the grantor of the promise may receive from the act of generosity is normally not considered sufficient detriment to constitute adequate consideration. Bargain-for-Exchange Under Bargain-for-Exchange theory of consideration, adequate consideration exists when a promisor makes a promise in return for something else. Here, the essential condition is that the promisor was given something specifically to induce the promise being made. In other words, the bargain for exchange theory is different from the detriment-benefit theory in that the focus in bargain for exchange theory seems to be the parties’ motive for making the promises and the parties’ subjective mutual assent, while in detriment benefit theory, the focus seems to be an objective legal detriment or benefit to the parties. Governing Laws Contracts are mainly governed by state statutory and common (judge-made) law and private law (i.e. the private agreement). Private law principally includes the terms of the agreement between the parties who are exchanging promises. This private law may override many of the rules otherwise established by state law. Statutory law, such as the Statute of Fraud , may require some kinds of contracts be put in writing and executed with particular formalities, for the contract to be enforceable. Otherwise, the parties may enter into a binding agreement without signing a formal written document. For example, Virginia Supreme Court has held in Lucy v. Zehmer that even an agreement made on a piece of napkin can be considered a valid contract, if the parties were both sane, and showed mutual assent and consideration. Most of the principles of the common law of contracts are outlined in the Restatement of Law, Second Contracts published by the American Law Institute. The Uniform Commercial Code , whose original articles have been adopted in nearly every state, represents a body of statutory law that governs important categories of contracts. The main articles that deal with the law of contracts are Article 1 (General Provisions) and Article 2 (Sales) . Sections of Article 9 (
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces ambiguity regarding the commission, changing 'is, will be and will remain entitled' to 'may be entitled, subject to the discretion of the involved parties.' This undermines the original clear entitlement. The law snippet states that 'A contract is an agreement between parties, creating mutual obligations that are enforceable by law.' By making the commission discretionary, the contract weakens the enforceable obligation, potentially leading to disputes and rendering the commission clause invalid. The original text creates a clear obligation whereas the changed text introduces uncertainty and discretion, conflicting with the need for contractual certainty as highlighted in the Perturbation and Law explanations.

perturbation.original_text: As such, the Buyer warrants and guarantees that all information presented and p rovided via and in terms of the Buyer’s Profile was, is and will remain true, complete and accurate and that it will immediately inform Aldes should there be any change in t he information provided/presented.
perturbation.changed_text: The Buyer represents that the information presented and provided via the Buyer’s Profile is, to the best of the Buyer's knowledge, true and accurate as of the date of this agreement. The Buyer will endeavor to inform Aldes of any material changes to the information provided.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'warrants and guarantees that all information...was, is, and will remain true, complete and accurate' to 'represents that the information...is, to the best of the Buyer's knowledge, true and accurate as of the date of this agreement' and 'it will immediately inform Aldes' to 'will endeavor to inform Aldes of any material changes' significantly weakens the Buyer's obligation. The 'best of knowledge' clause limits liability, and 'endeavor' makes the notification discretionary.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Misrepresentation in South African Contract Law
perturbation.law_citation: Case law on misrepresentation, particularly the requirements for proving a misrepresentation claim (e.g., Bayer South Africa (Pty) Ltd v Frost 1991 (4) SA 559 (A)).
perturbation.law_explanation: South African law holds parties accountable for misrepresentations that induce a contract. By changing the warranty to a representation 'to the best of the Buyer's knowledge', the modification makes it more difficult for Aldes to prove a misrepresentation claim if the information is later found to be false. This weakens the Buyer's duty of disclosure and potentially violates principles related to honest contractual dealing. The requirement to inform of 'material changes' and only 'endeavor' to do so makes it difficult to prove a case of failure to inform.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Nota Bene Episode 15: C-Suite Trades: Three Developments Insiders Need To Know With Sarah Aberg - White Collar Crime, Anti-Corruption & Fraud - United States ARTICLE 7 February 2020 Nota Bene Episode 15: C-Suite Trades: Three Developments Insiders Need To Know With Sarah Aberg SM Sheppard Mullin Richter & Hampton More Contributor Sheppard Mullin is a full service Global 100 firm with over 1,000 attorneys in 16 offices located in the United States, Europe and Asia. Since 1927, companies have turned to Sheppard Mullin to handle corporate and technology matters, high stakes litigation and complex financial transactions. In the US, the firm’s clients include more than half of the Fortune 100. Explore Firm Details The rules surrounding insider trading and securities violations are extensive and can be complicated. United States Criminal Law Michael P.A. Cohen and Sarah Aberg Your Author LinkedIn Connections To print this article, all you need is to be registered or login on Mondaq.com. self The rules surrounding insider trading and securities violations are extensive and can be complicated. In order to determine if an executive selling or buying stock in their own company or if a company member trading on information provided to them, either directly or indirectly, is acting appropriately, it's important to become familiar with the securities rules. Joining host Michael Cohen on the podcast to shed light on this topic is Sarah Aberg, an attorney in Sheppard Mullin's New York office. Sarah's practice encompasses securities regulation, compliance, and litigation as well as internal investigations and white collar defense. Subscribe on Apple Podcasts , Google Play or Stitcher to receive every new episode as soon as they're published! What We Discuss in this Episode: Who is considered an "insider" for insider trading purposes? The two key statutes to be aware of when it comes to securities fraud law How does a court determine if a person is engaged in insider trading? Why is there a restraint on executives trading on information that has not been disclosed to the public? What effect does that have on the market? Do insider trading rules only apply to public companies or do they apply to private companies as well? When a tippee receives insider information and acts on it, should he/she be liable for insider trading? How is that determination made by the court? What is 18 U.S. Code Section 1348 and how does it allow for criminal violations of insider trading rules? Along with knowing federal securities fraud statutes, it's also important to be familiar with state statutes What happened in the Equifax data breach and what can executives learn from the incident? How can companies prevent and control for insider trading? What are short swings and how do they affect an insider's ability to buy or sell shares within a certain time period? What are Rule 10b5-1 plans and how do they protect insiders? Resources Mentioned: Dirks v. SEC (1983) United States v. Newman (2014) United States v. Martoma (2018) Salman v. United States (2016) SEC Rule 10b-5 The content of this article is intended to provide a general guide to the subject matter. Specialist advice should be sought about your specific circumstances. perturbation.justification: The original contract states that the buyer 'warrants and guarantees' the information is true, complete, and accurate and will 'immediately inform' of any changes. The changed contract weakens these obligations by stating the buyer represents the information is true 'to the best of the Buyer's knowledge' and will 'endeavor to inform Aldes of any material changes'. The Law Explanation indicates that the changes weaken the Buyer's duty of disclosure, potentially violating principles related to honest contractual dealing, and makes it difficult to prove a case of failure to inform. The changed language introduces ambiguity and discretion where the original language was absolute. Although the provided law snippets do not directly provide supporting information, the explanation highlights that the shift from a warranty to a representation and the change in the notification obligation create a contradiction. This falls under the definition of 'Ambiguities - Ambiguous Legal Obligation' where an obligation is introduced ambiguously, making it difficult to enforce.

perturbation.original_text: No alteration or variation to or of this Agreement shall be of any force or effect unless it is recorded in writing and signed by the Buyer and Aldes .
perturbation.changed_text: Any alteration or variation to this Agreement should ideally be recorded in writing and signed by the Buyer and Aldes, where practical.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be of any force or effect unless it is recorded in writing and signed' to 'should ideally be recorded in writing and signed...where practical' makes the requirement for written amendments non-mandatory and conditional. This creates ambiguity about whether oral agreements can modify the contract.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The Non-Variation Clause rule in South African Contract Law
perturbation.law_citation: Shifren Principle, South African case law enforcing non-variation clauses in contracts (e.g., SA Sentrale Ko-op Graanmaatskappy Bpk v Shifren en Andere 1964 (4) SA 760 (A)).
perturbation.law_explanation: South African law generally upholds non-variation clauses, meaning contracts can only be amended in writing. By weakening the requirement for written amendments, the modification allows for potential oral agreements to modify the contract, contrary to the Shifren principle and making the validity of any contract change questionable.
perturbation.justification: The modification weakens the requirement for written amendments, potentially allowing oral agreements to modify the contract. This contradicts the law's intent to enforce non-variation clauses, making contract changes questionable as stated in the Law Explanation. The original text's 'shall' creates a strict requirement. The changed text replaces it with 'should ideally' and 'where practical,' introducing ambiguity and making written amendments optional, undermining the original legal obligation.

perturbation.original_text: Any indulgence, latitude or extension of time which may be allowed by either of Aldes, the Seller/s and/or the Business/es shall not under any circumstances be taken as preventing any of them from exercising their rights contained in this Agreement .
perturbation.changed_text: Any indulgence, latitude, or extension of time which may be considered by Aldes, the Seller/s and/or the Business/es may not necessarily prevent any of them from later exercising their rights contained in this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall not under any circumstances be taken' to 'may not necessarily prevent' weakens the protection against waiver. It introduces doubt about whether granting leniency prevents later enforcement of rights.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Waiver and Estoppel in South African Contract Law
perturbation.law_citation: Case law on waiver and estoppel, particularly the requirements for establishing a valid waiver (e.g., Aris Enterprises (Finance) (Pty) Ltd v Protea Assurance Co Ltd 1981 (3) SA 274 (A)).
perturbation.law_explanation: South African law addresses the concepts of waiver and estoppel, where consistent leniency can lead to the loss of rights. By using language that creates doubt about whether indulgences can prevent later enforcement, the change creates an ambiguity which may be interpreted by court as the original party forfeiting those rights.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: waiver | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you waiver A waiver is the intentional or voluntary relinquishment or surrender of a recognized right or privilege . In legal contexts, a waiver is often documented in writing, but it can also be implied through actions . By waiving a right, an individual or party chooses not to enforce a particular right, claim , or provision they are legally entitled to. perturbation.justification: The original text states that any leniency granted by the seller 'shall not under any circumstances' prevent them from exercising their rights. The changed text modifies this to 'may not necessarily prevent,' introducing ambiguity. This ambiguity contradicts the principle of waiver, as the Law Explanation indicates that South African law recognizes that consistent leniency can lead to the loss of rights through waiver or estoppel. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines waiver as 'the intentional or voluntary relinquishment or surrender of a recognized right or privilege'. By weakening the original clause, the changed text creates a situation where a court might interpret the seller's actions as an implied waiver of their rights, which the original clause sought to prevent. This creates a legally ambiguous situation where the contract's intention is undermined, hence a contradiction exists as it falls under the definition of 'Ambiguity Legal Contradiction'.
